content.text: Class Components and Why Function Components Won
From 2013 to 2019, class components were the only way to write a React component that had local state or lifecycle behaviour. Since React 16.8 introduced hooks in February 2019,
function components with hooks are the recommended approach for all new code
. Understanding why the community switched — and what class components look like — equips you to read legacy codebases and understand the design decisions that hooks were solving.

The same component in modern React:
Class components expose lifecycle hooks by overriding specific methods on the class. The three most important were:
After first render (component appears in DOM)
Before component is removed from DOM
Before render — return false to bail out
When a child component throws (error boundary)
Error Boundaries are the one surviving reason you might still write a class component today —
has no hooks equivalent as of React 19. In practice you write one ErrorBoundary class component, often copied from the docs or a library like

Every event handler in a class component required either a
call in the constructor or an arrow class field. This was a constant source of bugs for developers coming from other languages and for beginners. Function components have no

With hooks, each concern is fully co-located in its own

3. Reusing Stateful Logic Required Awkward Patterns
In the class component era, sharing stateful logic between components required one of two patterns —
. Both work, but both add indirection and make the component tree harder to read.
With hooks, extract a custom hook — a plain function, no wrapper component, no component tree pollution:
4. Class Components Are Verbose by Design
A minimal class component requires a constructor,
method — around 10 lines of boilerplate before any business logic. The equivalent function component is 2 lines.
The Stale Closure Problem (Function Components vs Class)
Function components read state through closures — each render creates a fresh snapshot of all variables. This is an important difference from class components, where
always points to the current instance.
This closure behaviour in function components is actually

in most cases — each render's handlers see the state that was present when the user triggered the action. The class behaviour of reading current state inside old handlers can cause different, subtler bugs. When you need the latest value inside an async callback, use
How to Read Class Components Today
You will encounter class components in older codebases, open-source libraries, and Stack Overflow answers. Recognising the patterns is more important than being able to write them from scratch:
→ runs after every update; check if relevant props/state changed before acting (=
→ the JSX the component produces (= the return value of the function)
Deprecated Lifecycle Methods to Recognise and Avoid

Three lifecycle methods were deprecated in React 16.3 and removed in React 18 due to incompatibility with concurrent rendering. If you see them, the codebase is at least 5 years old and likely needs updating:
Error Boundaries: The Class Component You May Still Need
Error Boundaries are class components that catch JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree. They are the only feature that still requires a class component as of React 19:
In practice, most teams use the
component so they never have to write a class component themselves.
When You Must Maintain a Class Component
If you join a team with a codebase still using class components, you have two options:
If a file uses class components, add to it using class component patterns. Mixing both styles in one file is confusing.

Class and function components can live in the same tree — you can rewrite a component from class to function without touching its parent or children. This is the recommended approach when you have test coverage.

The React team has committed to keeping class components working indefinitely. React 19 still supports them. You will never be forced to rewrite working class components — but all new code should use function components and hooks.

Testing: Class Components vs Function Components
The shift to function components also changed testing philosophy. In the class component era,
was the dominant testing library. Enzyme let you "shallow render" a component — rendering only the component itself without its children — and assert on internal state:
When hooks arrived, Enzyme's shallow rendering broke for many hooks-based components. The community shifted to
, which never accesses internal state — it tests the component the way a user would interact with it:

RTL works equally well for class and function components, but its philosophy maps far more naturally to hooks-based code — both focus on observable behaviour rather than internal implementation details.
to enable shallow prop and state comparison before re-rendering. This is the class equivalent of
For more granular control, class components could implement
to skip the render entirely. The function component equivalent is to wrap specific expensive computations in
rather than gating the entire render.
Why Concurrent Mode Broke Class Component Assumptions

React 18's concurrent mode revealed a fundamental mismatch with class components. In concurrent mode, React can start rendering a component, pause it, then resume later — or even discard the work and start over. This means a component's lifecycle methods may fire multiple times before the component actually appears on screen.
Class components that perform side effects in

or relied on render running exactly once per commit were broken by this. React 18 introduced StrictMode's double-invoking of render and effects specifically to surface these bugs — but the deeper issue is that the class lifecycle model assumes synchronous rendering, which concurrent React does not guarantee.
Function components with hooks are explicitly designed for this world: hooks do not run during the render phase (they set up after it), and
's cleanup-then-rerun model handles React pausing and resuming correctly.

Before class components fell from favour, a Babel transform (now a standard JS proposal) allowed class fields syntax that reduced some of the boilerplate. You may see this in code written between 2018 and 2021:
This is syntactic sugar — the underlying class mechanics (prototype,
, lifecycle) remain unchanged. Arrow class fields attach the function to the instance rather than the prototype, which is why
is bound correctly. The tradeoff: each instance gets its own copy of the function rather than sharing one via the prototype chain.
Class components were the only option for stateful React from 2013 to 2019.
They had three major problems: confusing
binding, lifecycle methods that split related logic, and no clean way to reuse stateful logic.
Hooks (React 16.8, 2019) solved all three. Function components became the recommended approach for all new code.
Class components still work in React 19 and Error Boundaries still require them.
Knowing the lifecycle → hooks mapping lets you read legacy code and rewrite it incrementally.
